step_by_step_thinking: The question asks about the location caused by embrasure formation due to divergence from the contact areas in proximal surfaces. Embrasures refer to spaces occurring naturally between adjacent teeth due to differences in wear patterns or developmental grooves. Divergence from the contact areas means the proximal surfaces do not align perfectly, creating gaps. Given this context and considering the anatomy and physiology of the oral structures mentioned in the articles, embrasures typically form **lingually** because the crowns generally have a lingual inclination compared to their mesio-distal orientation, especially noticeable in molars. Hence, they would create more space (embrasure) on the lingual side when there is any misalignment.
raw_response:
The question asks about the location caused by embrasure formation due to divergence from the contact areas in proximal surfaces. Embrasures refer to spaces occurring naturally between adjacent teeth due to differences in wear patterns or developmental grooves. Divergence from the contact areas means the proximal surfaces do not align perfectly, creating gaps. Given this context and considering the anatomy and physiology of the oral structures mentioned in the articles, embrasures typically form **lingually** because the crowns generally have a lingual inclination compared to their mesio-distal orientation, especially noticeable in molars. Hence, they would create more space (embrasure) on the lingual side when there is any misalignment.
\boxed{B} Based on the evidence presented, the correct option is B: Lingually. Embryonic divergence leading to embrasures tends to develop more frequently on the linguo-cervical region due to inherent structural characteristics like cervical convexities and lingual inclinations typical in tooth morphology.
